text: In comparison to dorsal rectopexy, be it a posterior sutured rectopexy or a dorsal mesh rectopexy, ventral rectopexy has been found to give better functional outcomes in terms of constipation scores as well as in terms of quality of life. The reason for this improved functional outcome is that in the ventral rectopexy, the rectal ampulla is not mobilized and the lateral ligaments are not divided as we do in the dorsal rectopexy. This mobilization of the ampulla and division of the lateral ligaments has been found to increase GI transit time and worsen constipation. In ventral rectopexy, the lateral and posterior mobilization of the rectum is avoided. Thereby, the chance of autonomic nerve injury with subsequent urinary and sexual dysfunction is minimal. The rectal prolapse always starts with the internal interception. The interception always starts in the anterior wall of the rectum. The ventral rectopexy addresses the lead point of the interception better. Also, most patients with rectal prolapse have a deep cul-de-sac into which they can have an enterocele containing the small bowel or the sigmoid. This is also treated during ventral rectopexy unlike in dorsal. Likewise, the ventral rectopexy can be used to correct simultaneous vaginal prolapse as well as pelvic floor descent.

The surgeon positions standard laparoscopic ports for rectal surgery and performs an initial assessment of the pelvis, noting a deep cul-de-sac and thickened peritoneum. The lateral peritoneal fold of the rectum is opened to enter the DME plane, revealing the inferior mesenteric vessels anteriorly and hypogastric nerves posteriorly. The dissection is limited to the retrorectal space to expose the sacral promontory, proceeding in a cranio-caudal fashion along the lateral peritoneal fold to the anterior rectum at the cul-de-sac, without dividing the lateral ligaments.

During the anterior dissection for ventral rectopexy, the surgeon carefully exposes the anterior rectal wall, dissecting posterior to the rectovaginal septum in women and posterior to the dental villus fascia in men. The dissection is performed meticulously to avoid injury to the pelvic plexus of nerves and the thickened, scarred tissue of the anterior cul-de-sac. The lower limit of dissection reaches the pelvic floor muscles and perineal body, creating a tunnel for mesh placement without lateral dissection.

The surgeon performs anterior rectal dissection, identifying the lower limit approximately three to four centimeters from the anal verge using a per-rectal examination. A proline mesh, measuring fifteen by five centimeters, is prepared for placement. The mesh is fixed to the anterior rectal musculature at the lowermost point of mobilization, avoiding entry into the rectal lumen. Sutures are placed tangentially, securing the mesh to the anterior rectal wall, with additional sutures taken higher up to the lateral rectal wall and mesorectum to distribute tension. The procedure is performed laparoscopically, with the surgeon navigating the limited space within the tunnel.

The surgeon fixes the upper end of the mesh to the sacral promontory using permanent metal tackers and transfacial sutures, ensuring the mesh is taut but not overly tight to prevent altering the anorectal angle. The mesh is positioned in the gutter created by the lateral peritoneal incision. The peritoneum is then closed over the mesh using a V-lock suture to reconstitute the cul-de-sac, preventing mesh exposure and bowel adhesions.

To conclude, ventral rectopexy offers better functional outcomes compared to dorsal rectopexy. It also addresses the other problems of the anterior compartment such as walled prolapse, pelvic floor descent and enterocele. In addition, it avoids autonomic nerve injury with urinary and sexual dysfunction.
